questions.prompt: Complete: "The bus ___ stops outside the school runs every fifteen minutes."

options.text: who
where
whose
that

questions.explanation: A bus is a thing and the subject of "stops", so use that (or which): the bus that stops outside. "who" is only for people, "where" introduces a place inside the clause (not the subject), and "whose" shows possession and needs a noun after it.

questions.prompt: Complete: "She married a chef ___ restaurant is famous across the whole city."

options.text: who
which
whose
who's

questions.explanation: whose shows possession (his restaurant): a chef whose restaurant is famous. "who" needs a verb directly after it (a chef who cooks), "which" is for things not people, and "who's" means "who is" — a completely different meaning.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This is the city ___ I was born."

options.text: that
which
who
where

questions.explanation: Use where for a place when the meaning is "in which": the city where I was born. "that" and "which" can refer to the city as an object but not as a location without a preposition (the city in which I was born), and "who" is only for people.

questions.prompt: In which sentence CAN the relative pronoun be omitted?

options.text: The journalist who broke the story works here.
The report that she wrote won an award.
I found the cat that was hiding in the garden.
The man whose wallet I found lives nearby.

questions.explanation: You can drop the relative pronoun only when it is the object — a subject follows. In the second option "she" follows "that", making it the object: the report she wrote. In the first and third options a verb follows (broke, was hiding), so the pronoun is the subject and must stay. In the fourth option "whose" is possessive and can never be dropped.

options.text: The colleague that helped me is on holiday.
The street which I grew up is very quiet.
The letter which arrived this morning is for you.
Is this the bag that you were looking for?

questions.explanation: The error is in the second option: "the street which I grew up" is wrong — which can't carry the meaning of location here. It should be the street where I grew up (or in which I grew up). The other three correctly use that for a person and a thing, which as the subject of a thing, and that as the object of a thing.

questions.prompt: Combine into one sentence: "I spoke to a manager. The manager's team had won the award."

options.text: I spoke to a manager who team had won the award.
I spoke to a manager which team had won the award.
I spoke to a manager whose team had won the award.
I spoke to a manager that team had won the award.

questions.explanation: whose shows possession (the manager's team): a manager whose team had won. "who" alone needs a verb after it (not a noun like team), "which" is for things, not people, and "that" cannot be possessive — none of them can replace "the manager's" except whose.
